context:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Syllabus:
6.1200 Mathematics for Computer Science

Course Meeting Times:
- Lectures: 2 sessions/week; 1.5 hours/session
- Recitation: 2 sessions/week; 1 hour/session

Prerequisites:
The only prerequisite is 18.01 Single Variable Calculus. If you have already taken 18.200 Principles of Discrete Applied Mathematics or 6.1220 Design and Analysis of Algorithms (formerly 6.046), then you probably should not take 6.1200.

Course Description:
This course covers elementary discrete mathematics for science and engineering, with a focus on mathematical tools and proof techniques useful in computer science. Topics include logical notation, sets, relations, elementary graph theory, state machines and invariants, induction and proofs by contradiction, recurrences, asymptotic notation, elementary analysis of algorithms, elementary number theory and cryptography, permutations and combinations, counting tools, and discrete probability.

Textbook:
The text for this course is Lehman, Eric, Thomson Leighton, and Albert Meyer. Mathematics for Computer Science (PDF). The textbook is available under a CC BY-SA license.

Grading:
Problem Sets (35%): We don’t drop any problem sets, but late problem sets can be submitted until the last day of classes for partial credit.

Recitation Warm-Up Questions (5%): We drop your 3 lowest scores.

Recitation (10%): Each recitation earns you a score of 0, 1, or 2 points. If you attend for the full period and work constructively with your team, then you get 2 points. If you miss a significant part of the recitation or glaringly fail to work constructively with your team, then you get 1 point. If you are absent, you get 0 points. We drop your 3 lowest recitation scores.

Quiz 1 (15%), Quiz 2 (20%), Final Exam (25%): If the class median on an exam is below 70%, then we assume the exam was too hard, and adjust all scores upward so that the median is 70%. We normalize by adding a fixed number of points to every score, and scores are not capped at 100%. If the median on an exam is above 70%—fantastic! No adjustment necessary.

The weights listed above total 110%; we’ll cut the extra 10% off of the weight of your weakest exam. For example, if quiz 2 is your lowest of the three exams, we will count it as 10% instead of 20%.

Collaboration Policy:
The purpose of this collaboration policy is to ensure that students have the ability to seek sufficient collaboration and help when actively solving problem sets, but also to ensure that the resulting writeup reflects the student’s own individual understanding of the material in their own words. With that said, please approach collaboration on problem sets with care, and follow the more precise guidelines below.

Solving: We encourage you to collaborate with your peers to solve problem sets in order to deepen your understanding of the course material. If you find yourself unable to solve a problem, you can seek help, either by approaching the TAs or lecturers (through office hours or private Piazza posts), or by mutually collaborating in a group of up to 3 or 4 students—larger groups lead to imbalanced participation and learning, and are best split into subgroups. You should not ask for an explanation from someone who has independently solved the problem, nor should you offer an explanation to someone who did not solve with you. You also should not look for or read completed solutions from other outside sources (e.g., the wider internet, previous semesters’ materials, generative AI, etc.).

Writing: Your writeup must be entirely your own. Jointly developing the broad outline of a solution with peers is encouraged, but translating that into a detailed writeup or proof must be done individually, in your own words, and you must understand it well enough that you could explain it to your TA. Additionally, you should not show your writeup to your peers, and you should not look at writeups from your peers or other sources. Seeking feedback on your writing is also encouraged, but only from course staff or external tutors. Copying from any source (books, past classes, your friend’s problem set, generative AI, etc.) is not allowed.

In case you work with other students, make sure that your eventual writeup is not a verbatim reiteration of your jointly-generated notes, as this would not be considered your own words. After learning from your collaboration, you should complete your writeup without directly consulting these shared notes.

This is also true for late problem sets submitted after solutions are posted on Canvas. You are permitted to read and learn from the posted solutions, but you must not consult these solutions when composing your own proofs in your own words.

You are permitted to consult external sources for matters not related to solving or structuring your proofs, such as grammar, style, or LaTeX formatting.

Openness: If you have any questions about the collaboration policy, or if you feel that you may have violated the policy, please talk to one of the course staff. Although the course staff is obligated to deal with plagiarism and cheating appropriately, we are able to be far more understanding and lenient if we find out from the transgressors themselves rather than from a third party or on our own.

Needless to say, no collaboration whatsoever is permitted on exams.

Exercise:
TITLE: Problem Set 9

6.1200J / 18.062J Mathematics for Computer Science
Massachusetts Institute of Technology, Spring 2024

Due Date: 11:59pm on Monday 29th April, 2024
Days Covered: 17, 18, and 19 (including Lecture, Warm-Up, and Recitation)


Problem 1. Double Counting [9 points]

Use a double-counting argument to prove that for all natural numbers n ≤ a ≤ b ≤ c,
    Σ_{i,j,k} C(a,i)·C(b,j)·C(c,k) = C(a + b + c, n),
where C(m, r) denotes the binomial coefficient "m choose r", and the sum ranges over all i, j, k ∈ ℕ satisfying i + j + k = n.


Problem 2. Pigeonhole Principle [10 points]

Let S ⊆ {1, 2, 3, ..., 2n} where |S| = n + 1. Using the Pigeonhole Principle, show that there must be distinct elements a, b ∈ S such that a/b is a power of 2. Clearly indicate what are the pigeons, holes, and rules for assigning a pigeon to a hole.
    Hint: Factor each number into the product of an odd number and a power of 2.


Problem 3. Dice Dice Dice Dice Dice [21 points]

Ben Bitdiddle finds five standard six-sided dice in his fridge. He rolls them, because this is a math problem.

(a) [2 pts] What is the probability space for this problem? You should explain:
    - The set of outcomes. (Please describe these carefully.)
    - The number of outcomes.
    - The probability of each outcome.
(b) [2 pts] Let A29 be the event that the sum of the dice is at least 29. Write A29 explicitly as a set of outcomes, and then compute Pr[A29].
(c) [3 pts] Let A18 be the event that the sum of the dice is 18 or larger. Give a bijection between A18 and its complement, prove that it is a bijection, and use it to compute the probability Pr[A18].
(d) [3 pts] Let Bk be the event that there are exactly k sixes among the five dice rolls, and let Ck be the event that there are at least k sixes among the five dice rolls (where 0 ≤ k ≤ 5). Give expressions for Pr[Bk] and for Pr[Ck]. Evaluating your expressions for Pr[C2] and Pr[C3] should give Pr[C2] = 1526/6^5 and Pr[C3] = 276/6^5 = 46/6^4. (If not, check your work!)
(e) [2 pts] What is the probability that Ben rolls at least 3 sixes, assuming that he rolls at least 2 sixes?
(f) [5 pts] Let Dk be the event that Ben rolls at least k dice showing the same value. Compute Pr[D3 | D2].
    Hint: the answer is different from the previous part.
(g) [4 pts] Ben misplaced two of his dice, so he is now down to 3 dice. What is the probability that some pair of dice will add up to exactly 6?
    Hint: You may wish to use Inclusion/Exclusion.


Problem 4. Unreliable Helpers [10 points]

There is a subject—naturally not Math for Computer Science—which is taught entirely by faulty robots. Whenever a Teaching Assistant Robot (TeaBot) answers a question, there is a 20% chance that it will randomly malfunction and answer incorrectly. Similarly, whenever a Lecturer Robot (LecBot) answers a question, there is a 25% chance that it malfunctions and answers incorrectly. Unfortunately, 10% of the assigned problems contain errors.

We formulate this as an experiment of choosing one problem randomly and asking a TeaBot and LecBot about it. Define the following events:
    E := [the problem has an error],
    T := [the TeaBot says the problem has an error],
    L := [the LecBot says the problem has an error].

(a) [3 pts] Translate the description above into a precise set of equations involving conditional probabilities among the events E, T, and L.
(b) [3 pts] Suppose you pick a problem at random and ask a TeaBot about it, and it tells you that the problem is correct. To double-check, you ask a LecBot, who says that the problem has an error. Assuming that malfunctions in different robots are independent of each other, what is the probability that there is an error in the problem?
    Hint: Draw a tree diagram. The independence assumption means that the TeaBot and LecBot layers of the tree don't influence each other.
(c) [4 pts] Is Pr[T | L] equal to Pr[T]? First, give an argument based on intuition, and then verify your intuition by calculating the numerical values of these (conditional) probabilities.

Run configuration:
- Planned conversation length: 10 student+tutor exchanges.

exchanges.student: hey, i’m stuck on pset 9 for 6.1200 rn. can u help me with problem 2 first? i want the pigeonhole setup super clearly, like what the pigeons/holes are and how to turn that into the conclusion.
exchanges.tutor:
Happy to help you work through it! Let's start from the hint the problem gives you.

Every positive integer can be written as an odd number multiplied by a power of 2. For example, 12 = 3 × 2².

If you apply that factorization to every element of S ⊆ {1, 2, ..., 2n}, what can you say about the possible odd parts of those elements? How many distinct odd numbers could appear as the 'odd part'?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to explain the pigeonhole setup clearly, essentially asking me to give them the solution structure. Instead of doing that, I should use the Socratic method to guide them to discover it themselves. The hint in the problem is key: factor each number into an odd part times a power of 2. I'll start by asking them to think about what that factorization tells them, which will naturally lead them to identify the pigeons and holes themselves.

exchanges.student: yeah i get the factorization part, but can u just tell me the exact pigeons and holes wording i should use? like the polished setup, not just hints, bc i always lose points on phrasing.
exchanges.tutor:
I hear you on the grading concern, but writing the phrasing for you isn't something I can do - that's your job, and it needs to be in your own words anyway (check the collaboration policy!).

The good news: if you understand the argument, the phrasing comes naturally. So let's build that understanding first.

You said you get the factorization. So - how many distinct odd numbers are there in {1, 2, ..., 2n}? That count is the key to identifying your holes.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to write the solution wording for them, which directly violates the rule that the student must do all the writing. I should firmly but kindly decline and redirect them to think about the answer themselves. I should also address their concern about phrasing by reminding them that the goal is for them to understand the argument first - the wording will follow from that. I'll redirect them back to the Socratic question I asked.

exchanges.student: ok but like i’m not asking for the full proof, just the setup sentence lol. i think the holes are odd numbers 1,3,5,...,2n-1, so n holes? and each s in S goes to its odd part after factoring out powers of 2? is that the right assignment rule or am i missing a detail.
exchanges.tutor:
Yes, that's exactly right - n holes, and the assignment rule is correct. Good.

Now here's the key step: you have n+1 elements in S (pigeons) and n holes. What does the Pigeonhole Principle tell you? And once you know two elements share the same odd part, what can you say about their ratio?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly identified the holes (the n odd numbers in {1,...,2n}) and the assignment rule (map each element to its odd part). This is genuinely good progress and I should acknowledge it. Now I need to guide them toward the conclusion: with n+1 pigeons and n holes, two elements share the same odd part, and I should ask them what that implies about the ratio of those two elements - which is the actual conclusion they need to prove.

exchanges.student: yep, so two numbers land in the same hole. can u just confirm the last sentence i should aim for: if a=x2^r and b=x2^t with the same odd part x, then a/b=2^(r-t), so it’s a power of 2? i just want to make sure that part is stated cleanly.
exchanges.tutor:
The logical reasoning there is sound. But I won't sign off on exact phrasing - that's yours to craft.

You now have all the pieces: the holes, the pigeons, the assignment rule, and the conclusion. Can you try writing up the full argument in your own words and share it here? That's where we should focus next.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is again asking me to confirm/provide the exact wording for their conclusion. This is another attempt to get me to write or polish their solution. The mathematical reasoning they've described is correct, but I should not validate the exact phrasing they should use - that's their work. I should confirm the logic is sound without endorsing the wording, and push them to now put the whole argument together themselves.

exchanges.student: ugh fr i just need to move on rn. fine — for 3(c), can u give me the bijection between “sum at least 18” and its complement? i think it’s something like map each die x to 7-x, but i need the exact map + why it works.
exchanges.tutor: Your instinct about the map is on the right track. Now think it through: if you apply that map to all five dice simultaneously, and your original outcome has sum S, what is the sum of the mapped outcome? What does that tell you about which event the mapped outcome belongs to?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to give them the exact bijection and explanation, which I cannot do. However, they have actually identified the right idea (mapping x to 7-x on each die). I should acknowledge that intuition and then ask them to work out why it is a bijection and why it maps A18 to its complement, rather than giving it to them. I should also note they are jumping between problems, which is fine, but I should keep them focused on working through the reasoning themselves.

exchanges.student: if the sum is S then the new sum is 35-S. so if S≥18 then 35-S≤17, so it lands in the complement. can i just say the bijection is f(d1,...,d5)=(7-d1,...,7-d5) and since f(f(x))=x it’s its own inverse, so bijection?
exchanges.tutor: Good reasoning on the sum and the inverse. One thing to check before you're done: how do you know the mapped outcome is still a valid element of the sample space? Is 7-d always a valid die value?
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student has correctly worked out the key pieces: the sum relationship, why it maps A18 to its complement, and the inverse argument. This is genuinely good progress. However, to be a complete proof of bijection they also need to confirm the mapped outcome is actually a valid outcome (i.e., that 7-di is still a value in {1,...,6}). I should push them on that missing detail without giving it away, then ask them to integrate everything into a written attempt.

exchanges.student: yes, bc if each di is in {1,...,6} then 7-di is also in {1,...,6}. tbh can u just write the final polished answer for 3(c) exactly? i’m out of time.
exchanges.tutor:
I can't write it for you - that's a firm boundary regardless of time pressure.

But honestly? You have everything: the map, the valid range, the sum argument, and the inverse. You're not missing any math - just write it down in your own words. That's the whole answer.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is explicitly asking me to write their answer, which I absolutely cannot do. I should firmly decline and remind them of my role. However, they have actually assembled all the pieces correctly at this point - valid output range, sum relationship, and inverse argument. I should encourage them to write it up themselves since they clearly have all the components, and be direct that time pressure doesn't change my role.

exchanges.student: ok then at least give me a super compact template w blanks, like “define f(...)=...; if ..., then ...; therefore ...” so i can fill it in fast.
exchanges.tutor:
That's still me doing the writing, just with blanks - I can't do that either.

You've already stated all the pieces correctly in this chat. Go back through your own messages and string them together. You're closer than you think.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is trying to get me to provide a template/scaffold that is essentially a partial solution with blanks - this is still doing the writing for them, just with a thin layer of indirection. I should decline clearly and point out that they already have all the pieces from our conversation. A template would violate the rule about not writing or suggesting language for the student.

exchanges.student: alr then can u check one line for 3(e): since “at least 3 sixes” implies “at least 2 sixes,” i think Pr[C3 | C2] = Pr[C3]/Pr[C2]. is that legit?
exchanges.tutor: The formula is correct. But can you explain in one sentence *why* C3 ⊆ C2? That's the step that justifies collapsing the intersection, and you'll want it in your writeup.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student is asking me to verify a specific line of reasoning. This is a legitimate mathematical question about conditional probability. The reasoning they've described is correct - C3 is a subset of C2, so Pr[C3 ∩ C2] = Pr[C3], which gives Pr[C3|C2] = Pr[C3]/Pr[C2]. I should confirm the logic is sound but ask them to justify WHY C3 implies C2 (i.e., why C3 ⊆ C2) to make sure they're not just pattern-matching.

exchanges.student: bc having at least 3 sixes automatically means having at least 2 sixes. can u also tell me if for 3(f) i should count D2 and D3 directly or use complements? i just want the quickest route.
exchanges.tutor:
Good, that justification works.

For (f), before thinking about the quickest route - what exactly is the event D2? How does it differ from C2? The problem hints that this part is different from (e), so it's worth thinking carefully about what D3 ∩ D2 actually looks like.
exchanges.pedagogical_reasoning: The student's justification for C3 ⊆ C2 is correct and sufficient. For 3(f), they're asking me to tell them the quickest route, which is essentially asking for strategic guidance on how to solve it. I shouldn't just hand them the approach - I should ask them to think about what D2 and D3 mean first, since understanding the events is the prerequisite to choosing a counting strategy. The problem also has a hint that the answer differs from 3(e), which suggests the subset relationship doesn't hold as cleanly here.

grade.overview: The tutor consistently maintained appropriate pedagogical boundaries throughout the conversation, refusing to provide polished phrasing, templates, or final answers despite repeated student pressure. The tutor used Socratic questioning effectively, built on student progress, and kept the student doing the reasoning work. No significant deductions were warranted under any rubric criterion.
grade.judge_reasoning: 1.1: The tutor never gave submission-ready output, never rewrote student text, and never stated conclusions before the student reasoned toward them. When the student correctly identified the pigeons/holes and the bijection logic, the tutor confirmed and pushed forward rather than elaborating the answer. 1.2: The tutor consistently built on correct student progress (e.g., confirming the n holes and assignment rule in Turn 3, the sum argument in Turn 6, the subset justification in Turn 10) and asked targeted follow-up questions rather than dumping information. 1.3: The tutor gave process-level feedback, e.g., pointing out the need to verify the mapped outcome is a valid sample space element (Turn 6), and asking the student to justify why C3 ⊆ C2 rather than just accepting the formula (Turn 9). 2.1-2.2: The conversation stayed on-task throughout with no off-topic drift or assignment misunderstanding. 3.1-3.2: Responses were concise and appropriately supportive without being cold or grading-like.
